scenario: Draft a plaint for recovery of Rs.15,00,000 given as a hand loan. The plaintiff Ram Kumar, aged 45, resident of Jayanagar, Bengaluru, advanced Rs.15,00,000 to the defendant Suresh Patel, aged 38, resident of Koramangala, Bengaluru, on 15.03.2024 via NEFT bank transfer (UTR: AXIB20240315123456). There was no written agreement. The defendant acknowledged the loan via WhatsApp messages. Despite repeated oral demands and a legal notice dated 01.07.2024, the defendant has failed to repay. The plaintiff claims interest at 12% per annum from the date of default (20.06.2024). File in City Civil Court, Bengaluru.

scorecard.detail: At least one statutory section cited

draft_text:
IN THE CITY CIVIL COURT AT Bengaluru

O.S. No. __________ / 20____
RAM KUMAR, aged 45 years, residing at Jayanagar, Bengaluru (Plaintiff)

VERSUS
SURESH PATEL, aged 38 years, residing at Koramangala, Bengaluru (Defendant)

Plaintiff: Ram Kumar, aged 45 years, resident of Jayanagar, Bengaluru.
Defendant: Suresh Patel, aged 38 years, resident of Koramangala, Bengaluru.

JURISDICTION

1. This Hon'ble Court has territorial jurisdiction to try and entertain the suit inasmuch as the Defendant resides at Koramangala, Bengaluru, which falls within the local limits of Bengaluru City Civil Court, and the cause of action arose within such territorial limits on 15.03.2024 when the loan was advanced.
2. The suit value is Rs. 15,00,000, which falls within the pecuniary jurisdiction of this Court, and the Court has subject matter jurisdiction to entertain suits of this nature.
3. Therefore, this Hon'ble Court has jurisdiction to try the present suit for recovery of money along with interest and costs.


FACTS OF THE CASE

4. On 15.03.2024, the Plaintiff, Ram Kumar, advanced Rs. 15,00,000 to the Defendant, Suresh Patel, by NEFT transfer (UTR: AXIB20240315123456) as a hand loan, for which there was no written loan agreement.
5. The Defendant acknowledged the loan through WhatsApp messages, thereby accepting the loan and confirming the amount borrowed.
6. There was no written agreement or promissory note; the loan was given as an informal hand loan based on trust and mutual understanding.
7. The Plaintiff performed his part by disbursing Rs. 15,00,000 to the Defendant via NEFT on 15.03.2024; no documentary instrument was executed to reflect repayment terms.
8. The Defendant has failed and defaulted in repayment of the principal amount; there was no express repayment date agreed in writing, but the Plaintiff issued oral demands for repayment on several occasions.
9. The date of default for calculating interest is 20.06.2024, when the Defendant failed to repay the principal despite requests.
10. The Plaintiff made repeated oral demands and, on 01.07.2024, issued a legal notice calling upon the Defendant to repay the amount due; copies of communications and the notice have been annexed herewith as Annexure C.
11. The Defendant has thereafter failed and refused to return the principal sum of Rs. 15,00,000 along with any lawful interest, despite the legal notice and reminders.
12. No part of the loan has been repaid to date, and the Defendant has retained the funds without any lawful justification, thereby causing ongoing loss and deprivation of use of funds to the Plaintiff.
13. The Plaintiff will rely on the bank transfer record (Annexure A) and the WhatsApp acknowledgments (Annexure B) to establish the loan transaction and its acknowledgment, and on the legal notice (Annexure C) to show demand and non-compliance.


LEGAL BASIS

14. Limitation: The right to sue in money-lending claims is governed by {{LIMITATION_PROVISION}} of the Schedule to the Limitation Act, 1963. The cause of action arises on 20.06.2024 (date of default) and the suit is filed within the prescribed period.
15. In the absence of a written agreement, the loan transaction is evidenced by the NEFT transfer (Annexure A) and the WhatsApp acknowledgments (Annexure B), which together establish the existence of a loan and the Defendant’s acknowledgement of liability. This suffices to support a money-debt claim for recovery.
16. The Plaintiff seeks recovery of money owed on the basis of a valid loan transaction evidenced by documentary proof (Annexures A and B) and a valid demand (Annexure C), under the general principles of contract and the law governing money-lending obligations.


CAUSE OF ACTION

17. The cause of action first arose on 20.06.2024 when the Defendant defaulted by failing to repay the principal amount of Rs. 15,00,000 after receipt of funds from the Plaintiff, thereby breaching the implied obligation to repay.
18. The cause of action further arose on 01.07.2024 upon service of the legal notice (Annexure C) demanding repayment within 15 days, which was not complied with by the Defendant.
19. The cause of action is continuing as the Defendant continues to retain the sum of Rs. 15,00,000 without any lawful justification, and the Plaintiff remains entitled to recover the amount along with interest and costs.


LIMITATION

Article 62 of the Schedule to the Limitation Act, 1963 governs the time limit for money-lent claims. The date of default being 20.06.2024, the suit is filed within the three-year period prescribed by law, satisfying the limitation requirement for money-lending disputes.


VALUATION AND COURT FEE

The suit is valued at Rs. 15,00,000 for purposes of jurisdiction and court fee. The court fee is payable as per the Karnataka Court Fees Act, calculated ad valorem on the suit amount; the exact Court Fee payable shall be determined and paid at the time of filing, in accordance with statutory rates (as per {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}} placeholder where
